expanded_description: Understanding $sp$ hybridization, where one $s$ orbital and one $p$ orbital mix to form two equivalent $sp$ hybrid orbitals oriented $180^\circ$ apart, resulting in linear molecular geometry and enabling triple bond formation through unhybridized $p$ orbitals.

core_concept.principle: $sp$ hybridization occurs when an atom mixes one $s$ orbital and one $p$ orbital to form two equivalent $sp$ hybrid orbitals oriented linearly at $180^\circ$, leaving two unhybridized $p$ orbitals perpendicular to the hybrid axis for $\pi$ bonding.
core_concept.explanation: Atoms with two electron domains (either two bonds or one bond + one lone pair) undergo $sp$ hybridization to achieve linear geometry. The two $sp$ hybrid orbitals form $\sigma$ bonds (or hold lone pairs) along a straight line. The two remaining unhybridized $p$ orbitals are perpendicular to each other and to the $sp$ axis, allowing formation of up to two $\pi$ bonds (triple bond or two double bonds). This explains the structure of molecules like ethyne ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$), carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$), and beryllium chloride ($\text{BeCl}_2$).

image.alt: Diagram showing one s orbital and one p orbital mixing to form two sp hybrid orbitals at 180 degrees, with two unhybridized p orbitals perpendicular
image.caption: $sp$ hybridization: 1 $s$ + 1 $p$ $\rightarrow$ 2 $sp$ hybrids ($180^\circ$) + 2 unhybridized $p$ orbitals

sections.introduction: $sp$ hybridization occurs when an atom needs to form two equivalent bonding regions oriented $180^\circ$ apart. This requires mixing atomic orbitals to create hybrid orbitals with the correct geometry and bonding capability.

key_concept.definition: $sp$ hybridization is the mixing of one $s$ orbital and one $p$ orbital from the same atom to form two equivalent $sp$ hybrid orbitals oriented linearly at $180^\circ$.
key_concept.context: Occurs when an atom has 2 electron domains (steric number = 2); leaves 2 unhybridized $p$ orbitals available for $\pi$ bonding.

principles.statement: Mixing 1 $s$ + 1 $p$ orbital produces exactly 2 $sp$ hybrid orbitals; no orbitals are created or destroyed.
principles.application: Predicts the number of hybrid orbitals available for $\sigma$ bonding or lone pairs.
principles.example: Carbon in ethyne: $2s^1 2p^3$ $\rightarrow$ two $sp$ hybrids + two unhybridized $p$ orbitals.
principles.analogy: Like exchanging 2 quarters for 2 half-dollars; same total value, different form.

principles.statement: The two $sp$ hybrid orbitals orient themselves $180^\circ$ apart to minimize electron repulsion.
principles.application: Explains linear molecular geometry for molecules with 2 electron domains.
principles.example: $\text{BeCl}_2$: Cl-Be-Cl bond angle = $180^\circ$ due to $sp$ hybrid orbital orientation.
principles.analogy: Like two people standing back-to-back; maximum separation is straight line.

principles.statement: Atoms undergoing $sp$ hybridization retain two unhybridized $p$ orbitals perpendicular to the $sp$ axis and to each other.
principles.application: Enables formation of two $\pi$ bonds (triple bond or cumulative double bonds).
principles.example: Ethyne ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$): each C has 2 unhybridized $p$ orbitals forming 2 $\pi$ bonds for C$\equiv$C triple bond.
principles.analogy: Like a pole ($sp$ axis) with two flags ($p$ orbitals) sticking out perpendicularly.

characteristics: Shortest and strongest bonds when overlapping
Lowest energy hybrid orbitals (closest to nucleus)
classifications.behavior: High $s$-character pulls bonding electrons closer to nucleus, creating shorter, stronger bonds

examples.explanation: $sp$ C-H bond (106 pm) is shorter and stronger than $sp^2$ C-H (108 pm) or $sp^3$ C-H (110 pm)

classifications.value: $sp$ hybridization occurs when atom has exactly 2 electron domains
characteristics: Can be 2 bonds, or 1 bond + 1 lone pair
classifications.behavior: Count electron domains to determine if $sp$ hybridization is needed

examples.explanation: C has 2 double bonds = 2 domains $\rightarrow$ $sp$ hybridization
C has 1 single + 1 triple bond = 2 domains $\rightarrow$ $sp$ hybridization

classifications.value: Two unhybridized $p$ orbitals remain available for $\pi$ bonding
classifications.behavior: Unhybridized $p$ orbitals overlap sideways with partner atom's $p$ orbitals to form $\pi$ bonds

examples.explanation: 1 $\sigma$ bond ($sp$-$sp$ overlap) + 2 $\pi$ bonds ($p$-$p$ overlap) = C$\equiv$C triple bond

worked_examples.title: Determining $sp$ Hybridization in $\text{BeCl}_2$
worked_examples.problem: Determine the hybridization of beryllium in beryllium chloride ($\text{BeCl}_2$) and explain the bonding.

step_by_step.explanation: Be central with 2 Be-Cl single bonds; Be has incomplete octet (4$e^-$); no lone pairs on Be
step_by_step.calculation: Be: 2 valence $e^-$; both used in bonds

step_by_step.explanation: 2 bonding pairs + 0 lone pairs = 2 electron domains
2 domains requires 2 hybrid orbitals; 1 $s$ + 1 $p$ = 2 $sp$ hybrids
Be $sp$ hybrids overlap with Cl $3p$ orbitals to form 2 $\sigma$ bonds
$sp$ hybrids orient at $180^\circ$; linear molecular geometry

worked_examples.final_answer: Be in $\text{BeCl}_2$ is $sp$ hybridized (2 electron domains); forms 2 $\sigma$ bonds using $sp$ hybrids; linear geometry at $180^\circ$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number 2 requires $sp$ hybridization to achieve linear geometry.

worked_examples.title: Explaining Triple Bond in Ethyne
worked_examples.problem: Explain how $sp$ hybridization enables the formation of the C$\equiv$C triple bond in ethyne ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$).

step_by_step.explanation: Each C has 2 domains (1 C-H + 1 C$\equiv$C); steric number = 2 $\rightarrow$ $sp$ hybridization
C-C $\sigma$ bond: $sp$-$sp$ overlap; C-H $\sigma$ bonds: $sp$-$1s$ overlap
Each C has 2 unhybridized $p$ orbitals perpendicular to $sp$ axis; these overlap sideways
step_by_step.calculation: 2 $\pi$ bonds (one from each pair of parallel $p$ orbitals)

step_by_step.explanation: 1 $\sigma$ + 2 $\pi$ = triple bond
$sp$ hybrids at $180^\circ$; linear molecule

worked_examples.final_answer: Each C in ethyne is $sp$ hybridized; $sp$-$sp$ overlap forms C-C $\sigma$ bond; 2 unhybridized $p$ orbitals per C form 2 $\pi$ bonds; total = 1 $\sigma$ + 2 $\pi$ = triple bond; linear geometry
worked_examples.concept_applied: $sp$ hybridization leaves 2 unhybridized $p$ orbitals per atom, enabling triple bond formation (1 $\sigma$ + 2 $\pi$).

sections.key_concept: $sp$ hybridization produces linear electron geometry with bond angles of exactly $180^\circ$. This is the only hybridization that produces perfectly linear geometry around the central atom.

categories.description: Two electron domains arrange themselves $180^\circ$ apart to minimize repulsion.
categories.detailed_mechanism: $sp$ hybrid orbitals have lobes pointing in opposite directions; maximum separation is linear.

examples.explanation: C has 2 domains (2 double bonds); $sp$ hybridization; linear electron geometry
C has 2 domains (1 single + 1 triple); $sp$ hybridization; linear electron geometry

categories.description: For $sp$ hybridized central atoms, molecular geometry is always linear (no lone pairs on central atom in stable neutral molecules).
categories.detailed_mechanism: 2 bonding domains + 0 lone pairs = linear molecular geometry; lone pairs on $sp$ central atom rare/unstable.

examples.explanation: 2 bonds, 0 lone pairs on Be; linear molecule
2 bonding regions, 0 lone pairs on C; linear molecule

categories.description: $sp$ hybridization produces exact $180^\circ$ bond angles with no deviation.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Symmetrical linear arrangement; no lone pairs on central atom to compress angles.

examples.explanation: H-C-C angle = $180^\circ$; C-C-H angle = $180^\circ$
O-C-O angle = $180^\circ$ exactly

classifications.value: Central atom forms 2 $\sigma$ bonds using $sp$ hybrids
characteristics: No lone pairs on central atom

examples.explanation: C forms 2 $\sigma$ bonds (to O) using $sp$ hybrids; 2 $\pi$ bonds using unhybridized $p$

classifications.value: Central atom forms 1 $\sigma$ bond + has 1 lone pair (rare for $sp$)
characteristics: Would be linear molecular geometry if lone pair invisible
classifications.behavior: Less common; lone pair occupies one $sp$ hybrid orbital

examples.explanation: N has 2 domains (1 bond + 1 lone pair); $sp$ hybridized; linear

classifications.value: $sp$ is the only hybridization producing $180^\circ$ bond angles

examples.explanation: Measured angle $180^\circ$ $\rightarrow$ $sp$; $120^\circ$ $\rightarrow$ $sp^2$; $109.5^\circ$ $\rightarrow$ $sp^3$

worked_examples.title: Predicting Geometry of $\text{CO}_2$
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry and bond angle of carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$) using hybridization theory.

step_by_step.explanation: O=C=O; C central with 2 double bonds; no lone pairs on C
step_by_step.calculation: C: 4 valence $e^-$; all used in double bonds

step_by_step.explanation: 2 double bonds = 2 electron domains (multiple bonds count as 1 domain)
2 domains requires 2 hybrid orbitals; 1 $s$ + 1 $p$ = 2 $sp$ hybrids
$sp$ hybrids orient at $180^\circ$; 2 bonding domains = linear molecular geometry
Linear geometry has exact $180^\circ$ bond angle

worked_examples.final_answer: $\text{CO}_2$: C is $sp$ hybridized (2 domains); linear molecular geometry; O-C-O bond angle = $180^\circ$

worked_examples.title: Comparing Bond Angles in Hydrocarbons
worked_examples.problem: Compare the C-C-H bond angles in ethane ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$), ethene ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_4$), and ethyne ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$).

step_by_step.explanation: Each C has 4 single bonds = 4 domains $\rightarrow$ $sp^3$
Each C has 1 double + 2 single = 3 domains $\rightarrow$ $sp^2$
Each C has 1 triple + 1 single = 2 domains $\rightarrow$ $sp$
Hybridization determines bond angle; more $s$-character = larger angle

worked_examples.final_answer: Bond angles: ethyne ($sp$, $180^\circ$) > ethene ($sp^2$, $120^\circ$) > ethane ($sp^3$, $109.5^\circ$); hybridization determines geometry
worked_examples.concept_applied: Hybridization type directly determines bond angles: $sp$ = $180^\circ$, $sp^2$ = $120^\circ$, $sp^3$ = $109.5^\circ$.

sections.key_concept: Atoms undergoing $sp$ hybridization retain two unhybridized $p$ orbitals that are perpendicular to the $sp$ axis and to each other. These orbitals overlap sideways to form $\pi$ bonds, enabling triple bonds or cumulative double bonds.

components.definition: Unhybridized $p$ orbitals are atomic $p$ orbitals that do not participate in hybridization; they remain perpendicular to the hybrid orbital axis and form $\pi$ bonds through sideways overlap.
components.interpretation: The two unhybridized $p$ orbitals allow formation of 2 $\pi$ bonds, completing a triple bond (1 $\sigma$ + 2 $\pi$) or two double bonds.

image.alt: Diagram showing two unhybridized p orbitals on each carbon atom overlapping to form two pi bonds in ethyne
image.caption: Two unhybridized $p$ orbitals per C atom form 2 $\pi$ bonds perpendicular to $sp$ $\sigma$ bond axis

principles.statement: The two unhybridized $p$ orbitals are perpendicular to the $sp$ hybrid axis and perpendicular to each other ($90^\circ$).
principles.application: Ensures $\pi$ bonds are oriented correctly for maximum overlap without interfering with $\sigma$ bond.
principles.example: In ethyne, one $\pi$ bond is in the plane of the page, the other is perpendicular to the page.
principles.analogy: Like a cross (+) shape around a pole ($sp$ axis); two flags sticking out at right angles.

principles.statement: $\pi$ bonds form through sideways overlap of parallel unhybridized $p$ orbitals on adjacent atoms.
principles.application: Explains why rotation around triple bonds is restricted (would break $\pi$ overlap).
principles.example: C$\equiv$C triple bond: 2 sets of parallel $p$ orbitals overlap to form 2 $\pi$ bonds.
principles.analogy: Like shaking hands sideways ($\pi$) vs. shaking hands palm-to-palm ($\sigma$).

principles.statement: A triple bond consists of one $\sigma$ bond (from $sp$-$sp$ overlap) and two $\pi$ bonds (from $p$-$p$ overlap).
principles.application: Explains bond strength and length: triple bonds are shortest and strongest due to 3 bonding interactions.
principles.example: Ethyne C$\equiv$C: 1 $\sigma$ + 2 $\pi$; bond length 120 pm; bond energy 839 kJ/mol.
principles.analogy: Like three ropes tying two posts together; harder to break than one or two ropes.

worked_examples.title: Counting $\sigma$ and $\pi$ Bonds in $\text{HCN}$
worked_examples.problem: Determine the number of $\sigma$ and $\pi$ bonds in hydrogen cyanide ($\text{HCN}$) and identify the orbitals involved.

step_by_step.explanation: H-C$\equiv$N; C has 1 single bond to H + 1 triple bond to N; N has 1 lone pair
C: 2 domains (1 single + 1 triple) $\rightarrow$ $sp$; N: 2 domains (1 triple + 1 lone pair) $\rightarrow$ $sp$
step_by_step.calculation: Both C and N are $sp$ hybridized

step_by_step.explanation: Every single bond = 1 $\sigma$; every multiple bond = 1 $\sigma$; H-C (1) + C-N (1) = 2 $\sigma$ bonds
Triple bond = 2 $\pi$ bonds; C$\equiv$N has 2 $\pi$ bonds
Total: 2 $\sigma$ + 2 $\pi$ bonds

worked_examples.final_answer: $\text{HCN}$: 2 $\sigma$ bonds (H-C $s$-$sp$, C-N $sp$-$sp$) + 2 $\pi$ bonds (C-N $p$-$p$); both C and N are $sp$ hybridized; linear geometry
worked_examples.concept_applied: Triple bond = 1 $\sigma$ + 2 $\pi$; $sp$ hybridization provides 2 unhybridized $p$ orbitals for 2 $\pi$ bonds.

worked_examples.title: Explaining Restricted Rotation in Triple Bonds
worked_examples.problem: Explain why rotation around a C$\equiv$C triple bond is restricted, using orbital theory.

step_by_step.explanation: Triple bond has 2 $\pi$ bonds formed by sideways overlap of parallel $p$ orbitals
Rotating one end of molecule would misalign parallel $p$ orbitals
Breaking $\pi$ overlap requires significant energy (bond strength)
$\pi$ bonds require parallel $p$ orbital alignment; rotation destroys alignment
step_by_step.calculation: Triple bonds are rigid; no free rotation

worked_examples.final_answer: Rotation around C$\equiv$C is restricted because it would break the sideways overlap of unhybridized $p$ orbitals forming the 2 $\pi$ bonds; $\pi$ bonds require parallel orbital alignment
worked_examples.concept_applied: $\pi$ bonds restrict rotation because sideways overlap requires parallel orbital alignment; rotation breaks overlap.

sections.key_concept: Common molecules exhibiting $sp$ hybridization include those with triple bonds (alkynes, nitriles) or cumulative double bonds (carbon dioxide, allenes). Recognizing these patterns enables quick hybridization assignment.

process_flow.description: Locate the atom whose hybridization needs to be determined.
Count bonding regions (single/double/triple = 1 each) + lone pairs on central atom.
Confirm bond angles are $180^\circ$ (or close to it).
Check for triple bond or two double bonds (2 unhybridized $p$ orbitals used).

classifications.value: Carbon atoms in triple bonds are $sp$ hybridized

examples.explanation: H-C$\equiv$C-H; both C atoms $sp$ hybridized; linear molecule
$\text{CH}_3$-C$\equiv$C-H; triple bond C's $sp$; methyl C $sp^3$

classifications.value: Carbon and nitrogen in nitrile group are $sp$ hybridized
classifications.behavior: Both C and N in C$\equiv$N are $sp$ hybridized

examples.explanation: H-C$\equiv$N; C and N both $sp$; linear
$\text{CH}_3$-C$\equiv$N; nitrile C and N $sp$; methyl C $sp^3$

classifications.value: Central atom with two double bonds is $sp$ hybridized
classifications.behavior: Central atom with 2 double bonds uses $sp$ hybrids for $\sigma$ bonds, unhybridized $p$ for $\pi$ bonds

examples.explanation: O=C=O; C is $sp$; 2 $\sigma$ + 2 $\pi$ bonds
S=C=S; C is $sp$; linear analogue of $\text{CO}_2$

classifications.value: Be and Mg compounds with 2 bonds are $sp$ hybridized
classifications.behavior: Group 2 elements form 2 bonds using $sp$ hybrids

examples.explanation: Cl-Be-Cl; Be is $sp$; linear; Be has 4 valence $e^-$

worked_examples.title: Hybridization in Allene ($\text{H}_2\text{CCCH}_2$)
worked_examples.problem: Determine the hybridization of each carbon atom in allene ($\text{H}_2\text{C=C=CH}_2$).

step_by_step.explanation: Two terminal C atoms with 2 H each + central C with 2 double bonds
Each terminal C has 1 double bond + 2 single bonds = 3 domains $\rightarrow$ $sp^2$
Central C has 2 double bonds = 2 domains $\rightarrow$ $sp$
Central C is linear ($180^\circ$); terminal C's are trigonal planar ($120^\circ$)
Different atoms in same molecule can have different hybridization based on their bonding

worked_examples.final_answer: Allene: terminal C atoms are $sp^2$ hybridized (3 domains each); central C atom is $sp$ hybridized (2 domains); central C is linear, terminal C's are trigonal planar
worked_examples.concept_applied: Each atom's hybridization is determined by its own electron domains; cumulative double bonds make central atom $sp$.

worked_examples.title: Hybridization in Azide Ion ($\text{N}_3^-$)
worked_examples.problem: Determine the hybridization of the central nitrogen in the azide ion ($\text{N}_3^-$).

step_by_step.explanation: Resonance structures: [N=N=N]$^-$ or [N$\equiv$N-N]$^-$ $\leftrightarrow$ [N-N$\equiv$N]$^-$
step_by_step.calculation: Central N has 2 bonding regions in all resonance forms

step_by_step.step: Count electron domains on central N
step_by_step.explanation: 2 bonding regions (double bonds or triple + single) = 2 domains
2 domains requires 2 hybrid orbitals; 1 $s$ + 1 $p$ = 2 $sp$ hybrids
$sp$ predicts linear geometry; azide ion is linear

worked_examples.final_answer: Central N in $\text{N}_3^-$ is $sp$ hybridized (2 electron domains); linear geometry; resonance doesn't change domain count
worked_examples.concept_applied: Resonance doesn't change hybridization; central N has 2 domains in all resonance forms $\rightarrow$ $sp$.

sections.key_concept: Misunderstandings about $sp$ hybridization can lead to incorrect structure predictions. Clarifying these concepts builds accurate understanding of linear geometry and triple bonding.

classifications.value: Assigning wrong hybridization to linear molecules
classifications.behavior: Remember: count electron domains on central atom only; 2 domains = $sp$

examples.explanation: Wrong: C is $sp^2$; Correct: C is $sp$ (2 domains on C, not O)
Wrong: C is $sp^3$ (4 bonds total); Correct: C is $sp$ (2 domains: 1 single + 1 triple)

classifications.value: Thinking all orbitals hybridize in $sp$ hybridization
classifications.behavior: Remember: $sp$ hybridization leaves 2 unhybridized $p$ orbitals for $\pi$ bonding

examples.explanation: Wrong: 3 $\sigma$ bonds; Correct: 1 $\sigma$ ($sp$-$sp$) + 2 $\pi$ ($p$-$p$)

classifications.type: Error: Counting Multiple Bonds as Multiple Domains
classifications.value: Counting each bond in double/triple bond as separate domain
classifications.behavior: Remember: multiple bonds count as 1 electron domain for hybridization

examples.explanation: Wrong: C has 4 domains (1 H + 3 N bonds); Correct: C has 2 domains (1 H + 1 C$\equiv$N region)

classifications.type: Error: Assuming All Linear Molecules are $sp$
classifications.value: Thinking linear geometry always means $sp$ hybridization
classifications.behavior: Diatomic molecules (HCl, CO) don't need hybridization; $sp$ applies to central atoms with 2 domains

examples.explanation: Linear but no hybridization needed (only 1 bond); $sp$ applies to central atoms with 2+ bonds

classifications.value: Determining hybridization of terminal atoms instead of central atom
characteristics: Analyzing O in $\text{CO}_2$ instead of C
Confusing which atom determines molecular geometry
classifications.behavior: Focus on central atom for molecular geometry; terminal atoms hybridization less critical for overall shape

examples.explanation: Wrong: O hybridization determines shape; Correct: C hybridization ($sp$) determines linear shape

sections.introduction: Test your understanding of $sp$ hybridization with these practice problems. Attempt each before reviewing the solution.

key_concept.definition: Practice reinforces understanding of $sp$ hybridization, linear geometry, and triple bond formation.
key_concept.context: Focus on counting electron domains and identifying unhybridized orbitals.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 1
worked_examples.problem: Determine the hybridization of the carbon atom in hydrogen cyanide ($\text{HCN}$).

step_by_step.explanation: H-C$\equiv$N; C has 1 single bond + 1 triple bond
2 bonding regions = 2 electron domains (triple bond counts as 1)
2 domains requires 2 hybrid orbitals; 1 $s$ + 1 $p$ = 2 $sp$ hybrids

worked_examples.final_answer: Carbon in $\text{HCN}$ is $sp$ hybridized (2 electron domains); linear geometry
worked_examples.concept_applied: 2 electron domains (regardless of bond order) = $sp$ hybridization.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 2
worked_examples.problem: How many $\sigma$ and $\pi$ bonds are in ethyne ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$)?

step_by_step.explanation: H-C$\equiv$C-H
step_by_step.calculation: 1 C$\equiv$C triple bond + 2 C-H single bonds

step_by_step.explanation: Every bond has 1 $\sigma$; 1 C-C + 2 C-H = 3 $\sigma$ bonds
Triple bond has 2 $\pi$ bonds; single bonds have 0 $\pi$

worked_examples.final_answer: Ethyne: 3 $\sigma$ bonds (1 C-C, 2 C-H) + 2 $\pi$ bonds (in C$\equiv$C); total 5 bonds

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 3
worked_examples.problem: Explain why $\text{CO}_2$ is linear but $\text{SO}_2$ is bent, using hybridization theory.

step_by_step.explanation: C has 2 double bonds = 2 domains; no lone pairs on C
S has 2 bonding regions + 1 lone pair = 3 domains
$\text{CO}_2$: $sp$ (2 domains); $\text{SO}_2$: $sp^2$ (3 domains)
Lone pairs on central atom affect domain count and hybridization

worked_examples.final_answer: $\text{CO}_2$ is linear (C is $sp$, 2 domains, 0 lone pairs); $\text{SO}_2$ is bent (S is $sp^2$, 3 domains, 1 lone pair); lone pair on S changes hybridization and geometry
worked_examples.concept_applied: Lone pairs on central atom count as electron domains and affect hybridization/geometry.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 4
worked_examples.problem: Which orbitals overlap to form the C-H bonds in ethyne ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$)?

step_by_step.explanation: C is $sp$ hybridized (2 domains)
H has only $1s$ orbital
C-H $\sigma$ bond forms from overlap of C $sp$ hybrid + H $1s$

worked_examples.final_answer: C-H bonds in ethyne form from overlap of carbon $sp$ hybrid orbital + hydrogen $1s$ orbital ($sp$-$1s$ $\sigma$ bond)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Hybrid orbitals on central atom overlap with atomic orbitals on terminal atoms to form $\sigma$ bonds.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 5
worked_examples.problem: Predict the hybridization of the central atom in $\text{BeCl}_2$, $\text{CO}_2$, and $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$.

step_by_step.explanation: Be has 2 single bonds = 2 domains; 0 lone pairs
C has 2 double bonds = 2 domains; 0 lone pairs
Each C has 1 single + 1 triple = 2 domains; 0 lone pairs
All have central atom with 2 electron domains

worked_examples.final_answer: All three molecules have central atoms with 2 electron domains; Be in $\text{BeCl}_2$, C in $\text{CO}_2$, and both C's in $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$ are all $sp$ hybridized with linear geometry
worked_examples.concept_applied: 2 electron domains on central atom always indicates $sp$ hybridization and linear geometry, regardless of bond type (single, double, or triple).

summary: $sp$ hybridization occurs when an atom mixes 1 $s$ + 1 $p$ orbital to form 2 equivalent $sp$ hybrid orbitals oriented $180^\circ$ apart (linear geometry), leaving 2 unhybridized $p$ orbitals perpendicular to the $sp$ axis. Occurs when central atom has 2 electron domains (steric number = 2). $sp$ hybrids form $\sigma$ bonds (or hold lone pairs); unhybridized $p$ orbitals form up to 2 $\pi$ bonds (triple bond = 1 $\sigma$ + 2 $\pi$; two double bonds = 2 $\sigma$ + 2 $\pi$). Examples: ethyne ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$, C$\equiv$C triple bond), carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$, O=C=O), beryllium chloride ($\text{BeCl}_2$, Cl-Be-Cl), hydrogen cyanide ($\text{HCN}$, H-C$\equiv$N). $sp$ hybrid orbitals have 50% $s$-character (highest of all hybrids), producing shortest, strongest $\sigma$ bonds. Bond angles are exactly $180^\circ$ (linear). Common errors include: confusing $sp$ with $sp^2$/$sp^3$, forgetting unhybridized $p$ orbitals (needed for $\pi$ bonds), counting multiple bonds as multiple domains (triple bond = 1 domain), and analyzing terminal instead of central atoms. Systematic approach: (1) draw Lewis structure, (2) count electron domains on central atom (multiple bonds = 1 domain), (3) 2 domains = $sp$ hybridization, (4) verify linear geometry ($180^\circ$), (5) identify unhybridized $p$ orbitals for $\pi$ bonding. Mastery enables prediction of linear molecular structures, triple bond formation, and bonding orbital composition.
